text: Chapter 7 builds out Bernstein's vocabulary-development apparatus, starting from the forward-motion rule of under-resolving the I chord (Don't overemphasize the I chord) and a specific harmonic device—inserting a tritone-distance ii-V over a resolving dominant, with the caveat that rhythmic placement of the substitution matters as much as its harmonic logic (Tritone ii-V substitution, Rhythmic placement of substitutions). Voice leading is framed conceptually via Jim Hall: each string is a singer in a choir to be given a beautiful melodic line (Voice leading as choir writing). The chapter's core pedagogical claim defines creativity operationally as recursive variation—ten ideas from one, then ten from each of those (Creativity as variation generation)—and then enumerates three named variation methods: ear-led free variation that preserves the line's overall structure (Open approach to variation), phrases built around a chosen target note from the chord (Target notes around a chord tone), and then around two simultaneous target notes (Two-note target practice). The chapter introduces a permissive chromatic stance—any note can approach any target, dramatized by the 'party for the target' analogy where diatonic notes are family and chromatic notes are friends and neighbors (Chromatic notes around target, Party-for-the-target analogy)—and ties this to the observation that a single pitch carries different flavors under different chords (A note's flavor depends on harmony). It closes with the characteristic guitar-specific move of shape-based idea generation—playing what you see inside a fingerboard shape and then evaluating by ear, distinguishing intentional phrasing from 'pressing the buttons' (Shape-based idea generation, Phrasing vs pressing buttons).
